subject: In re FTX Trading Ltd. – Customer Claims Bar Date Reminder
body:
Dear Customer:

We would like to remind you that the *FTX Customer Claims Bar Date is September 29, 2023, 4 PM United States Eastern Time (“Bar Date”)*. The Bar Date is the deadline to file proofs of claim, against the FTX Debtors, on account of Customer Claims. We recommend all customers with a customer claim to begin the claim process by logging in to the Customer Claims Portal at https://claims.ftx.com. If you have already filed a proof of claim and do not have any changes, then there is no need to file another claim.

Please note that the FTX Debtors have also requested KYC information as part of the customer claim process. As such, we urge you to begin the Know Your Customer (“KYC”) process. Please note that it is *NOT* mandatory for you to have a verified KYC status in order to submit a claim. If you wish to submit a customer claim, you may do so during your KYC process prior to the Bar Date. Additionally, you do not need to have your KYC process completed and verified by the Bar Date. The KYC review and verification process will continue after the Bar Date.

Note that if your account was previously frozen as a result of the cybersecurity incident, please refer to the separate communication sent on September 17, 2023.

Please visit the FTX support website at https://support.ftx.com to learn more about the customer claim process and the KYC process. You may also contact our customer support team via email ( support@ftx.com ( support@ftx.com?subject=&body= ) ) if you have trouble logging in to the customer claims portal.

Sincerely,

FTX

FTX Noticing

Kroll Restructuring Administration LLC, 55 East 52nd Street, 17th Floor , New York , NY 10055

subject: WHERE WAS I LOST FROM PAST 30 DAYS...
body:
(You are receiving this email because you enrolled in my Offensive Hacking Unfolded course)

—------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Hey pentesters,

This is Avinash back here!

As I said in my last email dated 17th April, in the upcoming days, I’ll be sending you some really useful resources, tips and lessons which I wish I knew when I started my career as a penetration tester.

In that email, I basically shared with you a special youtube video explaining how I would approach learning pentesting (if I forgot every single thing and had to start over)

And at the end of that email, I asked people to reply with YES if they want a detailed youtube video about the business skills that turns an average pentester to a professional world-class pentester.

I literally got MULTIPLE HUNDREDS of replies below that email xD

BUT THEN THERE WAS NO SIGN OF ME😱

I was completely inactive from all social media from past 30 days.


And here’s the reason…

As you probably know, I am working on creating my next course Web Hacking 360°.

That is the main reason I was not posting anything online from so many days.

I have made some really bold promises with that course. And as I create it, I realize that it's the hardest course I have ever worked on creating!

But also the best creation of mine.


Talking about the BUSINESS SKILLS YOUTUBE VIDEO which I promised…

It will come, but not right now.

I think the majority audience is just not ready yet to understand those things.

So, to clear up some basics, I have posted THIS video recently:

Click the image to view the video.

Here I explained the absolute basics of penetration testing within 20 minutes.

EVERY SINGLE PENTESTER NEEDS TO UNDERSTAND THIS.

After watching this video, you will be ready for my next video which will be about business skills in pentesting.

Reply back with YES if you would like me to share more behind the scenes as I move forward in the process of creating Web Hacking 360° :)

Kind Regards,

Avinash Yadav


ComproAvi School

subject: Re: [perl #46019] Documentation for 'srand' has a problem in code snippet
body: Quoth qtog3-epoawmh@perl.org: > "perldoc -f srand" (and consequently, perlfunc.pod) contains the following: > > `` > Note that you need something much more random than the default > seed for cryptographic purposes. Checksumming the compressed > output of one or more rapidly changing operating system status > programs is the usual method. For example: > > srand (time ^ $$ ^ unpack "%L*", `ps axww | gzip`); > '' > > The problem is that 'ps axww | gzip' returns the same output every time, at > least on my system: > > `` > ben@Tyr:~$ ps axww|gzip > gzip: compressed data not written to a terminal. Use -f to force compression. > For help, type: gzip -h > '' This is only the case if gzip's stdout is a terminal (as it says). If gzip is writing to a pipe (as it is in the srand docs) it is perfectly happy, at least on my system: ~% ps axww | gzip | cat # gzip output Ben
model_reason: Legitimate email from perl.org, discussing a bug report and code snippet.
